stories.headline: Rockies win series against Blue Jays in 10 innings behind Kyle Freeland, Tyler Freeman's walk-off hit
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies beat the Toronto Blue Jays 2-1 in 10 innings at Rogers Centre on Thursday, clinching the three-game series against the defending American League champions. Kyle Freeland, a Thomas Jefferson High School graduate, pitched five innings of one-run ball, while pinch-hitter Tyler Freeman drove in the winning run with a single off Brendon Little in the 10th.

The game featured a notable Colorado pitching showcase, with Freeland facing Blue Jays starter Kevin Gausman, a Grandview High School graduate. Gausman was dominant over six innings — allowing no runs, striking out 10 and walking none — extending his record streak of double-digit strikeout, zero-walk starts to open a season. Colorado reliever Tyler Rogers, a Chatfield High grad, also threw a scoreless seventh for Toronto.

The Rockies finished their opening road trip 2-4 after losing their first three games in Miami, all by one run. Freeman, just activated from the injured list, said the team's identity is one of grit. 'We are going to be gritty, and we are going to go to the last out of every game,' he said after the win.

stories.headline: Denver Nuggets clinch eighth consecutive NBA playoff berth after Phoenix Suns loss
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have secured a spot in the Western Conference playoffs for the eighth straight year after the Phoenix Suns lost Tuesday night, clinching Denver's postseason berth with six regular-season games remaining. The Nuggets currently sit fourth in the Western Conference standings.

Denver followed the clinching with a 130-117 road win over the Utah Jazz on Wednesday, extending their winning streak to seven games. Jamal Murray led all scorers with 37 points, making 10 of 16 three-point attempts in the first 90 seconds alone. Nikola Jokic added a triple-double with 15 points, 17 rebounds and 12 assists.

The Nuggets are dealing with injury concerns heading into the postseason, but Jonas Valanciunas stepped up in an emergency role, posting 13 points and six rebounds in 13 minutes. Playoff tickets went on sale Thursday, April 2, at 10 a.m. MT via Ticketmaster.

stories.headline: Nathan MacKinnon scores 50th goal of season; Avalanche without Cale Makar for several games
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche center Nathan MacKinnon scored his 50th goal of the season Wednesday night against the Vancouver Canucks, marking the sixth time in franchise history a player has reached the 50-goal mark. MacKinnon's milestone comes amid a strong late-season push by the Avalanche.

The achievement is tempered by a significant injury concern: defenseman Cale Makar will miss several games after leaving Monday's game against the Calgary Flames in the third period and not returning. Coach Jared Bednar confirmed the absence without providing a specific timeline. Makar is considered one of the top defensemen in the NHL and his absence will be felt as Colorado enters the final weeks of the regular season.

Bednar also expressed frustration with his team's overall performance despite the win, showing restraint but making clear he had concerns about the team's effort level in recent outings.

stories.headline: Governor Polis signs bill allowing medical marijuana use in some health facilities for terminally ill patients
stories.summary:
Governor Jared Polis has signed Senate Bill 7 into law, allowing terminally ill patients enrolled in Colorado's medical marijuana program to use cannabis products while admitted to participating health facilities. The law takes effect August 12, 2026.

The measure is narrower than earlier versions of the bill, which would have required facilities to accommodate marijuana use. Under the final law, each health facility may choose whether to opt in and must develop its own policies for possession, storage and administration of non-smokable products such as edibles, tinctures and capsules.

Facilities that decline to participate are protected from licensing or certification penalties by the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment. The law also includes provisions allowing facilities to suspend compliance if federal action — such as threats to Medicare or Medicaid funding — requires it.

stories.headline: Colorado business leaders' economic outlook improves slightly but remains negative, CU Leeds survey finds
stories.summary:
A University of Colorado Leeds School of Business survey of more than 200 Colorado business leaders found the second-quarter business confidence index at 41.9 out of 100, with 50 considered neutral. While still negative, the figure represents a 10-point improvement over the same period a year ago, when tariff fears and federal restructuring pushed the index to its lowest level since the 2020 pandemic.

The survey was conducted two days after American bombs struck Tehran, yet the negative outlook was less pronounced than expected. Economist Brian Lewandowski said business leaders appear to be adapting to a persistent environment of uncertainty. 'Business leaders are signaling that they are getting more used to operating in an environment where there is uncertainty,' he said.

Additional concerns highlighted in the survey include Colorado's shrinking labor force, potentially zero job growth in 2025 after anticipated federal data revisions, and the disruptive impact of artificial intelligence. Forty percent of respondents said AI and automation is transforming their workforce, with 69 percent reporting that AI has increased their productivity at least slightly.

stories.headline: Denver parking garage collapse at 3600 S. Yosemite St. displaces tenants, raises questions about building maintenance
stories.summary:
A parking garage at 3600 S. Yosemite St. in Denver collapsed last week, damaging several cars and forcing evacuation of the 10-story office building. Tenants have been unable to return, and the building's Miami-based owner, SF Partners, has provided little information about a timeline for reoccupying the property.

Denver's Community Planning and Development department ordered SF Partners to obtain an emergency permit for temporary shoring and stabilization of the garage. A Colorado licensed structural engineer and a city building inspector must sign off before displaced vehicles can be retrieved, a process that city officials said could take days or more.

Tenants described longstanding maintenance problems with the building, including a chronically broken elevator and apparent unpermitted construction work on the office floors and the garage entrance prior to the collapse. The building had not pulled a permit in nearly three years, according to city records. SF Partners, which purchased the building for $13.4 million in 2019, did not respond to multiple requests for comment.

stories.headline: Denver restaurant scene sees 29 openings and 9 closings in March, with suburban growth outpacing city
stories.summary:
Denver's restaurant industry logged 29 openings and nine closings in March 2026, but more than half of the new spots debuted in the suburbs while seven of the nine closures were within Denver city limits. Industry operators cited the city's permitting process as a significant deterrent to opening new restaurants inside Denver proper.

Notable new Denver openings include FiNO on East Colfax Avenue, an expanded Ma's Kitchen on Colfax, and Chicken Riot on East Sixth Avenue. Suburban highlights include Metro Pie Pizza in Lakewood and Poulette Bakeshop's expanded location in Parker. Westword's monthly roll call also noted the debut of McGregor Square Food & Drink at 1601 19th Street, adjacent to Coors Field.

The Barn co-owner Matt Cianni, who opened in Englewood, said he deliberately chose a location 'as close to Denver as possible without being in Denver,' citing frustrations with post-COVID city permitting. City officials have launched streamlining initiatives in response to such complaints, but Westword reported no documented success stories from those programs so far.

stories.headline: Denver Botanic Gardens' tulip display canceled this year due to fungal blight; won't return until 2029
stories.summary:
The Denver Botanic Gardens' annual tulip display — typically featuring 10,000 to 15,000 blooms in the annuals garden each April and May — will not appear this year after a fungal disease called tulip fire destroyed the bulbs. Gardens spokeswoman Erin Bird said the blight was likely introduced through infected bulbs from a European vendor in fall 2023 and progressed significantly in 2024 and 2025.

Horticulturalists and plant experts recommend waiting three years before replanting in blight-affected soil, even if the soil is replaced entirely. As a result, the Gardens will not replant tulip bulbs in the annuals garden until fall 2028, with blooms not expected to return until spring 2029.

Visitors will find thousands of daffodils, hyacinths and lilies in the annuals garden this spring instead, and the Gardens noted that an unusually warm March has pushed multiple other plant varieties — including lilacs and peonies — into simultaneous bloom. 'It has been a really unique spring,' Bird said. 'You get to see everything in its peak all at once.'

stories.headline: March 2026 recorded as hottest March in Denver weather history
stories.summary:
March 2026 has been confirmed as the hottest March ever recorded in Denver, shattering previous heat records despite the month typically being the snowiest of the year. The city set two daily records for warmest low temperatures during the month: 54 degrees on March 25 and 51 degrees on March 30, according to National Weather Service data.

The record-warm month followed or accompanied significant snowfall, underscoring the volatile and extreme nature of Colorado's spring weather pattern. Denver has since returned to more seasonal conditions, with highs in the upper 50s and afternoon shower chances reported for early April.

The historically warm March aligns with broader trends reported earlier this week, including a pine beetle outbreak along the Front Range and elevated wildfire risk fueled by the record winter warmth.

stories.headline: Denver City Council again delays vote on replacing Lime and Bird scooters with Veo
stories.summary:
The Denver City Council's Transportation and Infrastructure Committee unanimously voted Wednesday to delay a decision on a proposed scooter contract with Veo for two weeks, pushing the vote to April 15. Council members said they needed more time to review the contract's details, including provisions for a low-income free-ride program currently serving about 8,000 Denver residents.

Veo would replace both Lime and Bird under the proposed agreement, offering a broader range of vehicles including seated scooters, two-person scooters, trikes and cargo bikes. The pricing structure would drop from 44 cents per minute to 25 cents per minute for Denver residents, though the $1 unlock fee would remain. The city would receive approximately $2.3 million annually under the deal.

Council President Amanda Sandoval said she did not receive the contract with enough lead time to fully analyze it. Councilman Chris Hinds suggested the delay also reflects broader council frustration with the Department of Transportation and Infrastructure following other recent decisions, including changes to a road diet on Alameda Avenue.

stories.headline: Denver officials and survivors launch Sexual Assault Awareness Month with art show and community event
stories.summary:
Denver city officials, law enforcement representatives and sexual assault survivors gathered Wednesday at the 40 West Gallery in Lakewood to mark the start of Sexual Assault Awareness Month and participate in the national 'Start by Believing Day' campaign. The Denver Police Department, Denver District Attorney's Office, Denver Health, the Blue Bench and the Rose Andom Center were among the organizations represented.

District Attorney John Walsh and forensic nurse program manager Michelle Metz emphasized the importance of believing survivors when they report assaults, citing statistics that up to 80 percent of sexual assaults go unreported. Denver Gazette reporting presented at the event showed that of 705 rape reports filed in Denver County in 2024, only 133 arrests were made — roughly one arrest per six reports.

The RISE Survivor Art Show, featuring 50 pieces created by sexual assault survivors, will be on display at the 40 West Gallery from April 3 through April 25 and is free to the public.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I've never been a cool kid. I wasn't even a cool adult. I'm just a lukewarm person in general.

topStory.headline: Trump Defends Iran War in Prime-Time Address as Oil Prices Rise, Hormuz Crisis Deepens
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump delivered a prime-time address to the nation on Day 32 of the U.S. war with Iran, defending the ongoing conflict as necessary for global security and pledging additional strikes over the next two to three weeks. Trump declared that Iran had been "eviscerated" and was "essentially no longer a threat," but stopped short of offering a definitive timeline for ending hostilities, saying operations would continue until military objectives were "fully achieved."

Military and foreign policy analysts offered a mixed assessment of the campaign's results. Retired Army Col. Steve Warren, an NBC News military analyst, described the effort as a tactical success but questioned whether it met the threshold of strategic success, noting that Iran may retain highly enriched uranium, control of the Strait of Hormuz, and the capacity to threaten regional allies. Former State Department official Aaron David Miller criticized Trump for failing to address the role of ground troops, ongoing indirect diplomatic negotiations, or potential mediators including Pakistan, Turkey, and Egypt.

The Strait of Hormuz has emerged as a critical flashpoint in the broader conflict. British Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper chaired a virtual meeting of nearly three dozen nations to coordinate efforts to reopen the waterway, which Iran has targeted in retaliation for the U.S.-Israeli military campaign. Cooper reported more than 25 attacks on vessels in the strait and approximately 20,000 seafarers stranded aboard 2,000 ships, and announced that military planners were being convened to examine de-mining operations and other defensive measures.

French President Emmanuel Macron publicly criticized the Trump administration's handling of the conflict, saying it was sending contradictory signals and lacked strategic coherence. "If you want to be serious, you can't say each day the opposite of what you said the day before," Macron said. The remarks came after Trump personally attacked Macron and accused France of failing to contribute to the war effort, comments Macron dismissed as unworthy of a response.

Oil prices climbed in the wake of Trump's address as markets absorbed the prospect of continued military operations and ongoing disruption to one of the world's most vital shipping corridors. The combination of unresolved questions about Iran's nuclear capabilities, no clear diplomatic off-ramp, and deteriorating relations between Washington and key European allies signals that the conflict's economic and geopolitical consequences are likely to intensify in the weeks ahead.
